speaker: interviewee
text: --18 Of December, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. And camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepa, you were telling us about your life in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got the flat in Clovelly. And we were-- there were not many refugee-- refugees or [INAUDIBLE] there. It was a block of eight flats, all Australian. And somehow they recognized that we were speaking French then at home, because Michael-- that was his language. And this-- they recognized French. So you know that they love all-- everything-- what-- anyway, in French. Anyway, making this short, the story, we got so friendly that I never had-- apart from Aunt Paula-- I never had friends so close and so devoted and so good as we had those neighbors. They were so helpful. I had an accident, straight two months after Australia, broke my back. And-- the spine. And I had to have an operation. They were night and day with me. They were going to King's Cross to buy French newspapers for me. They were really-- especially one couple. They had a daughter, two years younger than Michael, and they really were brought up together like brother and sister. And what was the best thing that in those days, kids, at 15, they left school and went to then jobs, or they learned something. Michael was the first one who went to high school. He went to the best schools here, because he was always top. So he went to Sydney High, which was then this school [INAUDIBLE] this. And of course, when they asked me what she's going to do, I told them, of course he will finish the school. He will go to university. That was something new for those Australian middle class-- lower middle class in Clovelly. But those kids took a cue from Michael, and they started to learn and to go higher. And this girl who went through high school with the help of Michael, and then she studied and-- it-- it was really-- that friendship lasts till today. I means-- they don't live anymore, only the daughter. She lives in Grafton, and we are still in-- very much in touch. [INAUDIBLE] and-- so that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Michael study at university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Architecture. And yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he married. And when-- he worked at the beginning, when he finished. He worked, at the beginning, at the-- with the government--

speaker: interviewer
text: Architects.

speaker: interviewee
text: --department of architecture. But not long. Then they left for overseas, like a honeymoon. And on the way home, they went to Israel. And in Israel was then after the Six Day War. So the beautiful life, the euphoria after the victory and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I must tell you one thing, that Michael hated Australia. Whenever we wanted to go-- not now, but when we came here-- whenever we wanted to go to the city, he always had one answer-- I don't like it. It is not Paris. You see? Now he comes to Israel, and it's engulfed with those cafes and the street and people day and night around, you know? He wrote me-- I have, still, those letters-- he wrote me, that is like Paris. I feel at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he stayed there. They stayed there eight years. The two-- my granddaughters, his daughters, were born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yael and Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Yael now is married with a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Married, and has a baby. So that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to finish up, your story has been absolutely incredible. And such stamina, to tell it. If you were going to tell your children or your grandchildren, great-grandchild, what was the reason for your survival, what would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you the story, because I don't think that somebody would believe me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the maternal grandfather, yes. We were very close. He was very traditional, as I told you, a religious Jew. And he loved to sit down with me, talk about-- read Talmud. He explained to me, because I learned in school, you know, and things like that. Now, as I told you, we were very close. Every Saturday he was coming to me for-- to-- for me to play for him. When he died, he had cancer. And of course, there was nothing to help him, not even to relieve his pain. I was working there. Then-- that were ration time-- 1940. My parents were not-- already not in Przemsyl. One day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they sent for me that my grandfather wants to see me before he dies. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I went there. There was my grandmother, sitting, crying. My aunt, who was in Przemsyl with her husband and their 16-years-old daughter, who was taken in the first roundup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the doctor was there, and the doctor pronounced my grandfather dead, and he left. And we were sitting there. My-- suddenly-- you see, I am embarrassed to tell it, because I am not religious. I don't believe in this thing. But I can't deny what I went through, what happened to me. Suddenly, my grandfather sat up in the bed, and he beckoned to me to come closer. When I come closer, he started to bless me for life. So my aunt started to cry, and she pushed her daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told, no. Nobody is for life. Only she. And-- and he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow that was always with me. Always, I could feel like there were so many situations which I was-- that I should have been dead. Like, would-- somebody would really watch and help me out from all those type situations. That was one thing. That was really in my subconscious afterwards, because whenever I came out of those tight spots, I remembered that. But-- so I did not think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the other thing is that I was very lucky with people. People just-- look, we were standing there, for example, in the camp at Plaszow the-- the one night before they took us in the-- it was a-- a transit camp, yes? From Warsaw. And there was nothing to eat. Of course, after two months in the cellars, we still did not-- suddenly came a Red Cross this-- truck. It was one truck for 100,000 people. I just stood there, and I looked. How could I approach it even? Approach even to this? So a German soldier comes to me and tells me, you stand here. I will bring you. And he brought me a roll and the apple for my child. And I was standing there. I did not need to push myself in, you see? So I was lucky. I was just lucky with people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow-- like, I would be guided to those sort of people, like, for example, [INAUDIBLE], where I was really like a member of family. For example, you notice that I don't pronounce well R. Especially in Polish, where the R is very sharp. That usually sounds strange. So you will never believe it, but the cousin [INAUDIBLE], who was a nurse in there, she had the same pronunciation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought sometimes that it was something-- like, I would be guided to a family, where my brother would look like one son, when my baby looked like the other son. I had the pronunciation like the cousin. You know? And it was everything-- that was one thing. And the other thing, you see, as I told you-- as-- as the German, the [GERMAN] told me, your behavior. I knew how to talk to them. I-- somehow-- I had always a good approach to people, you know? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did not behave-- like, for example, when her Jewish friends, Aunt Paula's Jewish friends, sometimes came to her, she sent me out, because she did not want me to meet them. You see? She was afraid of me. [LAUGHS] But then one day, a man came unexpectedly. His look was perfect. The minute he opens his mouth, that was it. He was a lawyer, as well. But his Polish was so distinctively Jewish that, when he-- he left, my aunt was very embarrassed, because that was the first time that I met one of her pre-war friends. And exactly a one like that, you see? So she asked me, what do you think about him? So I told her bluntly, he's a Jew. I don't know how he will survive. So she told me-- because the whole family, I must tell you that they were helping as much as they can. They were-- without money. They were helping to place the-- the the ones who survived the-- this uprising in ghetto if they could come through, you know. And so they were-- this-- this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very good. But then she was a bit embarrassed, and she told me that's his problem that he has to change all the time places from one place to another because of his speech. You see? And I was lucky with my impediment, you know? It was mine-- and so I found a family where that-- that was normal, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's one thing. And on the other thing-- on the other hand, as I told you, you just had to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. It was luck on one side. Perhaps the way that I, you know, connected with people, you know? I just want to finish up by asking you that it's now 50 years since the end of the war, since the Shoah. Do you have any message for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, all the messages we have, they fall on deaf ears. Look at Yugoslavia. Look at everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: We told, never again. Yes? We told that Holocaust, cleansing, racial hatred, should be erased. Yes? But it is not. It is everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism is rising the ugly head everywhere. This-- and if one generation more will forget what it was, it is a very big danger that it will repeat itself. And for the Jewish people, I think-- I have one message-- we are lucky that we have Israel now, because if we would have Israel then, a small piece of land, so many hundreds of thousands and millions would be saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to do everything to keep this small, little piece of homeland. To feel better-- better. That we-- well, I can't tell that 'we'-- 'I' did this, because I never went to Israel. But I do what I can, now, here and there, and those organizations to help it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that is the only message. The racial hatred which brings to such-- to such deeds, murderous deeds. And you still can't get people to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepa, we're going to end here. But we'd like to thank you very much for sharing this story with us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pepa, would you like to introduce your husband to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, Stasiek Rosenmann. We call him Stasiek. His full name is Anastasie and-- Rosenmann. We now-- we are married 55 years, but we knew each other before that, around five years before we got married. And all those dangerous and turbulent years, some, we went together. Some, we were separated. But after the war, we came together. Of course, we found each other, and we stayed together till today. Well, that tells a lot about him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- what do you want to know? He was in Poland. He studied law. And he was about to enter the law practice-- it means to stand for the last exam to the bar-- when the war broke out, when war broke out. Can I just ask Mr. Rosenmann-- you know your wife's story. What do you think of her strengths? Stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think she was very brave woman. And she-- and it helps that she was very intelligent. And in that situation, I don't know how somebody else would do the same thing that allowed her to survive in these-- in those circumstances. It's very hard to describe those circumstances [INAUDIBLE] to people who were not in Poland at the time and didn't go through all this and that Holocaust. They wouldn't understand. This pictures and stories told is very hard to tell everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much, both of you, for sharing this with us. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. That's the gathering of the family on occasion of my eldest sister's, Ella, wedding. And as she was the first one, [INAUDIBLE] everybody came here. There is an aunt from Czechoslovakia. There is an uncle from America. And my grandparents from both sides. And the whole family is there. That is why I thought that that is an important--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1928 or '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a-- a family holiday somewhere in a country. We often went to the villages around Przemsyl. There was a beautiful countryside, and some villages had accommodations, especially for those city folk to come to spend their holidays with children. That time, I see that-- usually my mother and father did not go with us. They just packed all the kids with [INAUDIBLE], and that was that. But that must have been a weekend, because my father is here and my mother is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I am standing behind-- behind my mother's this-- lounge? Or how-- 'deck chair,' really speaking. And I was there probably five, six years old. So what year does that make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would-- that has to be 1920, because my father is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's a cousin that's [INAUDIBLE]. Then is my sister. Behind her, her husband. And I am in the back. And then is my sister, who is Ruza, who lives now in Los Angeles. And in front of us is my-- our youngest brother, the blond one, who got me out from the ghetto afterwards, Henry. And his nanny, on the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been 1925, '26, because he was born in '21. And he looks to me like four or five years old. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Pepa.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a group from Hashomer Hatzair on one of our outings around Przemsyl. It was a-- and some of them I remember the names, but not all. I am in front, the first one. And the-- the others-- you want their names?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just what year do you think this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just an outing. We had very often this discussions or that. So we went outside, not always in a room, you know? We went outside. Or just for-- to hike or to-- to-- for a walk, or-- it was like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think it was, Pepa?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- it looks like '29. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my picture from our school photograph. That was in a school uniform just before my matriculation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a graduation of the top Hebrew students, because not the whole-- the whole class was not at the same level. So we were at the top level of the Hebrew class. And we graduated, in Hebrew, under the eyes of the rabbi from Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's a photo of graduation of the whole class. I must mention here that most of those friends from this class went, before the war, to Palestine. They did not stay in Poland. And I met them when I first went to Israel, 17 years after I was here in Australia. It means we did not see each other for 30-odd years. But they-- the-- the way that-- the reception there for me, it was something unbelievable. They come from all over Israel-- from Jerusalem, from this, from kibbutzim, from all parts-- just to meet me and to-- to-- to talk about our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people from this class survived, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] perhaps 20, or perhaps more. Because they were mostly in-- and I still have a few who are still alive, you know, there. Gradually, they went, you know, because at this age you can expect this. But at least 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Pepa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my youngest brother, Henry, the one who I was talking-- talking-- telling about that he was so blond and so blue-eyed and so Aryan-looking. And he got me out of the ghetto. And he was the one who was taking care of me till his death.

speaker: interviewer
text: This photo was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that must have been taken during the war, because you can see, still, a trace of a stamp on the photo. And he is in his leather jacket, which he wore during the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is after the war when we were living in Prague for a time being, just in transit. So that's my husband, on the right, my right. In the middle is Michael, who was then two, two and a half years old. Two and a half years old. He's curly-- curly-headed. And me, walking on the street of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaclavske Namesti.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- on the street of Prague, somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '46 the-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photo of my older granddaughter's wedding. And here you see, from left, my husband, the bridegroom, Yael-- the bridegroom-- David [INAUDIBLE], Yael. I am standing there, next. Michael is standing, and his other daughter, Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- what year she was married in? 1993? No, it's five.

speaker: interviewee
text: '93 or something. yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. And, as you see, my-- how-- I don't know how to call my granddaughter-- granddaughter's husband is a Lubavitcher. They turned very religious, which is something perhaps not new, but it is really a full circle from my grandfather, who was religious, through the two generations, who did not know-- who did not keep any tradition or religion, to the granddaughter, who came back to the grandparents' status. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that this is a photo of our great-granddaughter. Yael and David had a baby, [INAUDIBLE] Esther. And that are the two proud grand-- great--

speaker: interviewee
text: Great--

speaker: interviewee
text: --great-grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in August, '95?

speaker: interviewee
text: August, '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a birth certificate of my son, Michael, who was born in Warsaw in 1943. And it was issued by the parish of All Saints in Warsaw, and-- because that was the day he was born. And born then as a Catholic [INAUDIBLE]. And he is named here Lechoslaw Antoni Koralewicz, after my name-- Koralewicz. Mother, not married-- single mother. And the father, not known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is an extract from the birth certificate of this girl who lived in Krakow and who gave my brother that certificate on base of which I was-- I got the German-- by the which I got the German identity card, you know, in the name of Maria Koralewicz, who was born here and here in Krakow [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a certificate from the Jewish-- if we can call them 'authorities.' What is was is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Agency? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, no-- there was is a Jewish communal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Approved by the government.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, approved. And that is a certificate that I am Jewish. And that was issued after the war under my real name [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's the end of tape nine of the interview with Pepa Rosenmann. It's the 18 of December, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
